item.title: The asymmetric encryption
item.description:
To understand asymmetric encryption, it is important to know that two keys are used: a public key and a private key. The public key can be published without any special transmission method and is used to encrypt messages, which can then be decrypted using the private key. The private key must remain secret in order to decrypt messages encrypted with the public key. The distribution of the public key must be global to avoid forgeries.

Advantages

Advantages:

  1. Relatively high security.
  2. No key distribution problem.
  3. Capability of authentication with digital signatures.
  4. Unlike symmetric cryptography, PKC uses a fixed buffer size depending on the volume of data that can be encrypted and is not tied to streams.

However, PKC is more reliable and less susceptible to security breaches due to a wide range of possible encryption keys. PKC facilitates confidentiality, data integrity, and authentication, which form the parameters of key information (IA).

Disadvantages

Disadvantages of public-key systems include:

  1. Algorithms operate very slowly - roughly 10,000 times slower than symmetric ones. PKC is slower than secret key methods (or symmetric cryptography) due to high computational requirements.
  2. Large required key length.
  3. Problems with multiple recipients when the message needs to be further encrypted.
  4. Security risk available to every public key is also a disadvantage of public-key encryption systems.
